q: An organization mandates a baseline Definition of Done. The Scrum Team has agreed internally to apply stricter criteria. A new Developer joins and starts shipping work that meets the org DoD but not the team's stricter criteria. The Scrum Master should:
options: Coach the new Developer that the team's stricter DoD applies and is non-negotiable
Facilitate the team revisiting their stricter DoD with the new Developer as a full participant in the conversation
Allow the new Developer to follow only the org DoD; the stricter criteria are aspirational
Ask the Product Owner to enforce the team's DoD on the new Developer
why: The team's DoD belongs to the Developers as a whole, including its newest member. Treating it as a fixed rule the new Developer must accept undermines self-management; allowing it to be ignored undermines transparency. The Scrum Master facilitates a fresh conversation so the team can confirm or evolve the stricter criteria with all current members. The PO does not enforce DoD.

q: Two weeks into a one-month Sprint, the Developers find that the chosen technical approach is unworkable. The Sprint Goal is still feasible, but it now requires fundamentally different work than what is in the Sprint Backlog. The Scrum Master should:
options: Recommend the Product Owner cancel the Sprint and start a fresh one
Coach the Developers to renegotiate the Sprint Backlog with the PO while preserving the Sprint Goal
Direct the Developers to pivot to the new technical approach immediately
Note the issue and inspect it at the Sprint Retrospective
why: The Sprint Backlog is expected to adapt during the Sprint as more is learned. The Sprint Goal is the commitment, not the plan. Cancelling is for when the Sprint Goal becomes obsolete, which is not the case here. Directing violates self-management. Waiting for the Retrospective delays adaptation that the Sprint itself supports.

q: A senior stakeholder asks to attend the Daily Scrum to "stay informed about Sprint progress." The Scrum Master should:
options: Allow attendance because transparency to stakeholders is a core Scrum value
Decline; the Daily Scrum is a closed event for the Developers under Scrum rules
Coach the stakeholder on the Sprint Review as the right transparency forum, then decline
Allow with the constraint that the stakeholder cannot speak or interrupt the team
why: The Daily Scrum is for the Developers; its purpose is inspection and adaptation toward the Sprint Goal. Stakeholder presence chills team conversation and steers it toward reporting. Transparency to stakeholders is provided through the Sprint Review and visible artifacts (Sprint Backlog, Increment). The Scrum Master coaches on this rather than refusing on procedural grounds.

q: A Developer has missed Sprint Goals four Sprints in a row. Their teammates have tried to help; tension is rising; the Retrospective has not surfaced the issue. The Scrum Master should:
options: Wait for the team to raise it themselves — self-management requires patience
Address it privately with the underperforming Developer
Facilitate the next Retrospective so the team can inspect this team dynamic, even if uncomfortable
Take the issue to the Developer's line manager
why: The Scrum Master is accountable for the Scrum Team's effectiveness and for the productivity of Scrum events. Passive waiting does not honor that accountability when the Retrospective is repeatedly avoiding a clear issue. Private performance conversations and escalating to a manager bypass the Scrum Team's collective inspection — the team must own its dynamic, with facilitation.

q: Leadership announces a new performance review process based on individual story-point output. The Scrum Master should:
options: Communicate the new process to the team so they can adjust their workflows accordingly
Refuse to participate; this performance system fundamentally violates Scrum principles
Coach leadership on how individual-output metrics undermine self-management; propose outcome-based alternatives
Quietly comply with the rollout to maintain organizational support for Scrum overall
why: The Scrum Master serves the organization by leading and coaching on Scrum and empirical thinking. Individual output metrics distort behavior and erode self-management. Coaching with a constructive alternative (outcome measures) is more effective than communicating, refusing, or quietly complying — each of which abandons the SM's coaching accountability in different ways.

q: It is the scheduled Sprint Review and zero stakeholders attend. The Scrum Master should:
options: Cancel the Sprint Review and reschedule for a time when stakeholders can attend
Hold the Review with the Scrum Team and surface the absent stakeholders as an impediment to address
Skip the Sprint Review this Sprint and resume in the next one to save the team's time
Have the Scrum Master speak on behalf of stakeholders so the collaboration still happens
why: Scrum events are time-boxed and should not be skipped or rescheduled. Holding the Review preserves the team's inspection rhythm. The lack of stakeholders is itself a transparency and engagement problem the SM must surface — the team adapts the Product Backlog with the information it has, while the SM coaches the organization on the Sprint Review's purpose.

q: The Scrum Team has committed to a Sprint Goal that turns out to require security expertise the team lacks. A skilled external contractor is available immediately. Which actions are consistent with Scrum? (Select all that apply)
options: Renegotiate the Sprint Backlog scope and surface the skills gap as an impediment
Bring the contractor inside the Scrum Team for the Sprint as a Developer
Have the contractor work on the security task outside the Scrum Team for this Sprint
Ignore the gap; ship without security work and address it in a future Sprint plan
Begin cross-training Developers in the missing skill, even if the Sprint Goal is partially missed
why: Cross-functional means the team has the skills it needs each Sprint — but Scrum allows adapting the team and the Sprint to reality. Renegotiating scope and surfacing the gap (option 0), temporarily incorporating the contractor as a Developer (option 1), and using the Sprint to start building the skill internally (option 4) all preserve team accountability for the Increment. Treating the contractor as outside the team (option 2) creates a permanent dependency anti-pattern. Shipping without the security work (option 3) violates the Definition of Done.

q: Two Scrum Teams contributing to the same product have evolved different and incompatible Definitions of Done. A senior leader proposes that the Scrum Master "harmonize" the DoDs. The Scrum Master should:
options: Negotiate a common DoD with the other Scrum Master and impose it on both teams
Defer to the org-wide baseline DoD if one exists; otherwise let the two DoDs stand
Facilitate a working session with both teams' Developers to inspect the differences and converge on a unified DoD
Have the Product Owner select which team's DoD wins
why: For multiple teams on the same product, Increments must integrate, which requires a consistent DoD. The Definition of Done is created by the Developers — not by Scrum Masters, not by the Product Owner, and not by leadership. Imposing a DoD bypasses self-management. Allowing incompatible DoDs to stand breaks Increment transparency at the product level. Facilitating Developer-to-Developer convergence honors both principles.

q: Unable to reach the Product Owner during the Sprint, the Developers have been making product decisions in their absence — clarifying scope, making trade-offs, and reordering work mid-Sprint. The Scrum Master should:
options: Coach the Developers to stop making PO decisions and pause until the PO is reachable
Take over Product Owner activities and decisions until the PO returns to the team
Coach the PO on availability and facilitate a sustainable refinement rhythm with the team
Accept the situation; Developers can refine and order when the PO is unavailable
why: Developers can collaborate on refinement, but ordering and value decisions are PO accountabilities and cannot be delegated implicitly. Coaching the team to stop and wait creates blocked work. Taking over PO activities is not a Scrum role for the SM. Accepting Developers as substitute POs erodes the accountability structure. The principled fix is coaching the PO on availability — addressing the root cause.

q: After three Sprints of self-management, a Scrum Team is paralyzed: nothing gets decided, no one owns problems, Sprint Goals miss. They ask the Scrum Master to 'just tell us what to do.' The Scrum Master should:
options: Direct decisions for now — self-management can be reintroduced once delivery has stabilized
Refuse — self-management is non-negotiable and the team must figure it out themselves
Coach the team through specific decisions one at a time, building self-management capability gradually
Recommend the Scrum Team be disbanded and reformed with different membership
why: Self-management is foundational to Scrum, but it isn't binary — teams build it. Directing undermines the long-term goal and signals that compliance is acceptable. Refusing leaves the team without scaffolding. Disbanding is extreme. Coaching specific decisions — what does the team need to figure out next, and how — builds the muscle while keeping work moving.
